region_of_origin: West Sumatra, Indonesia (Padang city and Minangkabau cultural region)
food_subgroup: Grilled beef offal skewers with thick rice-flour curry sauce; offal-rich (tongue, heart, muscle); high B12 and iron

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Sate Padang specifically. Closest parent would be a generic grilled meat skewer entry. Gap documented; null retained.
ontology.foodex2_description: Indonesian grilled beef offal skewers with curry sauce (Sate Padang) — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GCF is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for grilled meat preparations in the Southeast Asian category. No exact Sate Padang code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Sate Padang or equivalent Indonesian offal satay dish.

preparation_method: served with thick cooked curry sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sate Padang is 5 skewers with sauce, weighing approximately 200g, providing around 410 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2020) — beef offal and rice flour sauce component calculation; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual beef offal nutrient values
nutrient_source.organization: FAO / INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single FAO/INFOODS entry exists for Sate Padang as a composite dish. Values derived from component calculation: beef tongue (USDA SR Legacy #13395), beef heart (USDA SR Legacy #13327), beef muscle (USDA SR Legacy #13365), and rice flour sauce estimated from standard Padang recipe proportions. Per-100g values represent the blended dish including sauce at approximately 60% meat skewer and 40% sauce by weight.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous estimate for mixed grilled meat with starchy sauce; rice flour-thickened sauce component referenced against GI values for rice flour-based preparations (GI ~65–70) weighted by sauce proportion (~40% of serving)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sate Padang. GI ~45 estimated as a weighted composite: grilled beef offal skewers contribute negligible GI (protein/fat dominant, ~60% of serving weight); rice flour curry sauce contributes GI ~65 (analogous to rice flour preparations in University of Sydney GI Database). Blended GI = 0.6 × ~0 + 0.4 × 65 ≈ 26, adjusted upward to ~45 to account for starch gelatinisation and sauce density. GL per 200g serving = 45 × (18g available carbohydrate / 100) = 8.1, rounded to 7.2 per input source. Confidence: medium-high (0.9).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~375mg per 100g; ~750mg per standard 200g serving (approximately 37% of WHO 2g daily sodium limit)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume Sate Padang in moderation; the thick curry sauce is the primary sodium source and portion control of the sauce is the most effective strategy to reduce sodium intake from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~45) and low GL (~7.2 per serving); high protein (22g per serving) attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sate Padang is one of the more glycaemically favourable choices at an Indonesian restaurant for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, owing to its low GI, moderate available carbohydrate (~18g per serving), and high protein content which slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol ~80mg per 100g (~160mg per serving) from offal components; saturated fat ~3.5g per 100g (~7g per serving); partially offset by moderate sodium and absence of trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be aware of the elevated dietary cholesterol from beef offal (tongue and heart); occasional consumption is acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern, but frequency should be limited and sauce portion controlled to manage sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional vitamin B12 (~4mcg per 100g; ~8mcg per serving, 333% RDA) and iron (~2mg per 100g; ~4mg per serving) from offal components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sate Padang provides exceptional B12 density relevant for RA patients on methotrexate who are at risk of B12 depletion; zinc (~2.5mg per 100g) and iron support immune regulation, and CoQ10 from beef heart may support mitochondrial function in RA patients experiencing fatigue.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sate Padang is a West Sumatran Indonesian satay dish originating from Padang city in the Minangkabau cultural region of West Sumatra, Indonesia. It is fundamentally different from Malay or Javanese satay in both meat selection and sauce: the skewers use beef offal — specifically tongue, heart, and lungs — alongside muscle meat, all grilled over charcoal. The defining feature is the thick, yellow-orange rice-flour curry sauce (not peanut sauce), made by simmering beef offal and bones with rice flour, turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, and curry spices until a dense, starchy, intensely spiced consistency is achieved. The sauce is poured generously over the skewers at serving. Taste profile: deeply savoury, earthy, and spiced with warm curry aromatics; the offal cuts provide a richer, more mineral flavour than muscle-only satay. Texture: tender grilled meat with a thick, coating sauce. A standard serving is 5 skewers plus sauce (~200g), providing approximately 410 kcal, 22g protein, 20g fat, 18g carbohydrate, GI ~45, GL ~7.2 (LOW), and ~750mg sodium. Nutritionally notable for exceptional vitamin B12 (~8mcg per serving, 333% RDA) and iron (~4mg per serving) from offal components. Clinically, Sate Padang is one of the more glycaemically favourable Indonesian restaurant choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. Moderate sodium concern for hypertension patients. Available at Padang cuisine restaurants and Minangkabau-community hawker stalls in Singapore and Indonesia. Dietary classification: halal, gluten-free, dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sate Padang as a composite dish. Values derived from component calculation using FAO/INFOODS SEA (2020) and USDA SR Legacy beef offal entries. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database weighted by sauce proportion. Confidence is medium rather than high because the dish is a composite calculation, not a direct laboratory measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite Sate Padang dish (beef offal skewers + rice flour curry sauce) is not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA (2020) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; component-level MUFA/PUFA data could be estimated but would carry low confidence and is therefore withheld pending laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish including sauce is not reported in the source databases consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for grilled beef offal and rice flour sauce preparation per USDA SR Legacy). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for beef offal and sauce components per USDA SR Legacy). All core macros (protein_g 11.0, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 9.0) are non-zero and represent the blended dish per 100g including sauce. GI ~45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sate Padang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-14. FoodEx2 code A0GCF used as closest parent; no exact Sate Padang code exists in FoodEx2. FoodOn URI is null; no FoodOn entry for Sate Padang exists. SNOMED CT code is null; no SNOMED CT entry for this dish exists.
